title: Atopic Dermatitis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Allergy/Immunology Fellows, Dermatology, Pediatrics, Primary Care
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of atopic dermatitis including barrier dysfunction and Type 2 inflammation
Apply diagnostic criteria and severity assessment for AD
Implement stepwise treatment approach per AAD 2024 guidelines
Select appropriate topical therapies (TCS, TCI, PDE4i, topical JAKi)
Identify candidates for systemic therapy and choose between biologics and JAK inhibitors

guidelineReferences.name: AAD Guidelines: Phototherapy and Systemic Therapies 2024
keyPoints: Strong recommendation for dupilumab, tralokinumab, abrocitinib, baricitinib, upadacitinib
Conditional recommendation for phototherapy, cyclosporine, methotrexate, azathioprine, mycophenolate

vignette.text:
A 28-year-old woman presents with a 20-year history of itchy, dry skin that started in infancy. She has tried multiple over-the-counter moisturizers and occasional OTC hydrocortisone without adequate relief. The itch significantly impacts her sleep and work productivity.

Past Medical History: Childhood eczema, allergic rhinitis, mild intermittent asthma

Family History: Mother with asthma, brother with eczema

Medications: Albuterol PRN, fluticasone nasal spray

Allergies: Dust mites (positive skin test)

physicalExam.general: Well-appearing woman, frequently scratching during interview

skin.face: Periorbital darkening (allergic shiners), infraorbital folds (Dennie-Morgan lines)
skin.flexures: Bilateral antecubital and popliteal fossae with erythema, lichenification, excoriations
skin.hands: Dorsal hand dermatitis with scaling and fissures

severity_assessment.DLQI: 18 (very large effect on quality of life)

questions.stem: This patient has features suggesting underlying barrier dysfunction and immune dysregulation.
questions.question: What is the pathophysiology of atopic dermatitis?

options.text: Pure barrier defect without immune involvement
Th1-predominant inflammation

Two key components: (1) BARRIER DYSFUNCTION: Filaggrin mutations (up to 50% of AD), decreased ceramides, impaired stratum corneum, increased allergen/irritant penetration, transepidermal water loss (TEWL); (2) TYPE 2 INFLAMMATION: Th2-skewed immune response, IL-4 and IL-13 (key cytokines - targets of biologics), IL-31 (pruritus), IgE elevation, eosinophilia; Creates itch-scratch cycle that perpetuates inflammation
Only IgE-mediated immediate hypersensitivity

questions.explanation: AD pathophysiology involves two interconnected components: EPIDERMAL BARRIER DYSFUNCTION: Filaggrin (FLG) gene mutations in up to 50% of AD patients. Filaggrin is crucial for stratum corneum integrity. Decreased ceramides and natural moisturizing factors. Increased transepidermal water loss (TEWL) → dry skin. Impaired barrier allows allergen/irritant penetration. TYPE 2 (Th2) IMMUNE DYSREGULATION: Th2-skewed immune response. Key cytokines: IL-4, IL-13 (both targeted by dupilumab), IL-31 (major driver of pruritus - targeted by nemolizumab), IL-5 (eosinophil activation). Results in elevated IgE, eosinophilia. ITCH-SCRATCH CYCLE: Scratching damages barrier further, promotes inflammation, releases more cytokines → more itch. This patient shows the classic atopic march: AD in infancy → allergic rhinitis → asthma.

sources.text: StatPearls: Atopic Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You want to confirm the diagnosis of AD.
questions.question: How is atopic dermatitis diagnosed?

options.text: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS - no definitive test; Hanifin-Rajka Criteria: MAJOR (need 3): Pruritus, Typical morphology/distribution (flexural in adults, facial/extensor in infants), Chronic/relapsing course, Personal/family history of atopy; MINOR (need 3): Xerosis, Elevated IgE, Early age of onset, Dennie-Morgan lines, Hyperlinear palms, Periorbital darkening; Key features: Age-appropriate distribution, itch, chronicity
Positive patch test confirms AD
IgE level makes the diagnosis
Skin biopsy is required

questions.explanation: AD is a CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS: HANIFIN-RAJKA CRITERIA (need 3 major + 3 minor): MAJOR FEATURES: (1) Pruritus - hallmark symptom, (2) Typical morphology and distribution: Infants: Face, scalp, extensor surfaces; Children/Adults: Flexural (antecubital, popliteal fossae), hands, neck, (3) Chronic or relapsing course, (4) Personal or family history of atopy (asthma, allergic rhinitis, AD). MINOR FEATURES (examples): Xerosis (dry skin), Elevated serum IgE, Early age of onset, Dennie-Morgan infraorbital folds, Hyperlinear palms, Periorbital darkening, White dermographism, Keratosis pilaris. This patient: Pruritus, flexural distribution, chronic course (20 years), personal/family atopy history, multiple minor features → clear AD diagnosis. No biopsy or lab test required for diagnosis (though IgE/eosinophils may support).

sources.text: StatPearls: Atopic Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has EASI 16 and significant QoL impact.
questions.question: How is AD severity assessed?

options.text: Severity assessment is unnecessary
IgE level determines severity

Multiple validated tools: EASI (Eczema Area and Severity Index): 0-7 mild, 7-21 moderate, 21-50 severe, >50 very severe; IGA (Investigator Global Assessment): 0-4 scale; BSA (Body Surface Area) involvement; Patient-reported: DLQI (quality of life), Pruritus NRS, POEM; Consider: Sleep impact, psychosocial effects, treatment history; This patient: EASI 16 (moderate), IGA 3 (moderate), but DLQI 18 (severe impact) → may warrant advanced therapy
Only BSA matters

questions.explanation: Severity assessment tools: CLINICIAN-ASSESSED: EASI (Eczema Area and Severity Index): Most widely used in trials, Assesses erythema, induration, excoriation, lichenification in 4 body regions, Score 0-72: Mild 0-7, Moderate 7-21, Severe 21-50, Very severe >50. IGA (Investigator Global Assessment): 0-4 or 0-5 scale, Global assessment. BSA (Body Surface Area): % of body involved. PATIENT-REPORTED: DLQI (Dermatology Life Quality Index): 0-30, >10 = very large effect, NRS Pruritus: 0-10 itch scale, POEM (Patient-Oriented Eczema Measure). IMPORTANT: QoL impact may exceed objective findings - consider both! This patient: EASI 16 (moderate), but DLQI 18 (severe QoL impact) and Pruritus NRS 8/10 → symptoms significantly impact life, may warrant systemic therapy despite 'moderate' EASI.

sources.text: StatPearls: Atopic Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You want to optimize topical therapy first.
questions.question: What topical therapies are used for atopic dermatitis?

options.text: FOUNDATION: Emollients/moisturizers - daily, liberal, essential for all patients; TOPICAL CORTICOSTEROIDS (TCS): First-line anti-inflammatory; Potency matched to site and severity (low for face/folds, higher for body); TOPICAL CALCINEURIN INHIBITORS (TCI): Tacrolimus, pimecrolimus; Steroid-sparing, good for face/sensitive areas; No skin atrophy; PDE4 INHIBITOR: Crisaborole - mild-moderate AD; TOPICAL JAK INHIBITOR: Ruxolitinib - mild-moderate AD, rapid itch relief
Moisturizers are optional
Topical steroids should be avoided
Only one topical agent should be used

questions.explanation: Topical therapy options: MOISTURIZERS/EMOLLIENTS: Foundation of AD management. Apply liberally, frequently (at least daily). Repair barrier, reduce TEWL. Continue even when skin clear. TOPICAL CORTICOSTEROIDS (TCS): First-line anti-inflammatory. Match potency to site: Low (hydrocortisone) for face, folds, genitals; Mid (triamcinolone) for body; High (fluocinonide) for thick skin, flares. Proactive use (2x weekly to prone areas) prevents flares. TOPICAL CALCINEURIN INHIBITORS: Tacrolimus 0.03%, 0.1%; Pimecrolimus 1%. Steroid-sparing, no skin atrophy. Excellent for face, periorbital, folds. boxed warning (theoretical lymphoma risk - not supported by data). CRISABOROLE (PDE4 inhibitor): approved ≥3 months. Mild-moderate AD. Stinging can limit use. RUXOLITINIB (topical JAK inhibitor): approved ≥12 years. Rapid itch relief. AAD recommends for mild-moderate AD.

sources.text: StatPearls: Atopic Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has moderate-severe AD inadequately controlled with topical therapy.
questions.question: When is systemic therapy indicated for AD?

options.text: Biologics are last resort only

Indications for systemic therapy: Moderate-to-severe AD (EASI >7) refractory to optimized topical therapy; Significant quality of life impact despite topicals; Large BSA involvement making topicals impractical; AAD 2024 STRONG recommendations: Dupilumab, Tralokinumab (biologics); Abrocitinib, Baricitinib, Upadacitinib (JAK inhibitors); CONDITIONAL recommendations: Phototherapy, Cyclosporine, MTX, AZA, MMF; AGAINST: Systemic corticosteroids (rebound, side effects)
Systemic therapy only for EASI >50
Prednisone is first-line systemic therapy

questions.explanation: When to consider systemic therapy: INDICATIONS: Moderate-to-severe AD refractory to optimized topical therapy, Significant impact on quality of life, sleep, work, Large BSA making topical therapy impractical, Failure of adequate topical trial. AAD 2024 RECOMMENDATIONS: STRONG FOR: BIOLOGICS: Dupilumab (IL-4/IL-13), Tralokinumab (IL-13); JAK INHIBITORS: Abrocitinib (JAK1), Upadacitinib (JAK1), Baricitinib (JAK1/2, off-label in US). CONDITIONAL FOR: Phototherapy (NB-UVB), Cyclosporine, Methotrexate, Azathioprine, Mycophenolate. CONDITIONAL AGAINST: Systemic corticosteroids - rebound flares, long-term side effects. This patient: EASI 16, DLQI 18, inadequate topical response → candidate for systemic therapy.

sources.text: StatPearls: Atopic Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You consider biologic therapy for this patient.
questions.question: What biologics are available for atopic dermatitis?

options.text: Biologics work within 24 hours

DUPILUMAB (Dupixent): First-line biologic (expert consensus); Anti-IL-4Rα (blocks IL-4 and IL-13); approved ≥6 months; Subcutaneous q2 weeks; No lab monitoring required; AE: Conjunctivitis (10-15%), injection site reactions; TRALOKINUMAB (Adbry): Anti-IL-13 only; approved ≥12 years; q2 weeks (can space to q4 weeks after response); Less conjunctivitis than dupilumab; LEBRIKIZUMAB: Anti-IL-13 (recently approved); NEMOLIZUMAB: Anti-IL-31 (pruritus-focused, approved 2024)
Omalizumab is first-line for AD
Monthly lab monitoring required

questions.explanation: Biologics for AD: DUPILUMAB (Dupixent): Mechanism: Anti-IL-4Rα monoclonal antibody, blocks both IL-4 and IL-13. First-line biologic per expert consensus. approved: ≥6 months for AD; also asthma, CRSwNP, EoE, prurigo nodularis. Dosing: Loading dose then 300mg SC every 2 weeks. No lab monitoring required. Side effects: Conjunctivitis (10-15%, can be managed), injection site reactions, peripheral eosinophilia. TRALOKINUMAB (Adbry): Anti-IL-13 only. approved ≥12 years. May have lower conjunctivitis rate. Can extend to q4 weeks after initial response. LEBRIKIZUMAB: Anti-IL-13, recently approved. Similar to tralokinumab. NEMOLIZUMAB: Anti-IL-31 receptor (pruritus cytokine). approved December 2024. Particularly effective for itch relief. Onset: Biologics typically show improvement in 2-4 weeks, with continued benefit over months.

sources.text: StatPearls: Atopic Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! DUPILUMAB (Dupixent): First-line biologic (expert consensus); Anti-IL-4Rα (blocks IL-4 and IL-13); approved ≥6 months; Subcutaneous q2 weeks; No lab monitoring required; AE: Conjunctivitis (10-15%), injection site reactions; TRALOKINUMAB (Adbry): Anti-IL-13 only; approved ≥12 years; q2 weeks (can space to q4 weeks after response); Less conjunctivitis than dupilumab; LEBRIKIZUMAB: Anti-IL-13 (recently approved); NEMOLIZUMAB: Anti-IL-31 (pruritus-focused, approved 2024).

questions.stem: The patient asks about oral options.
questions.question: What JAK inhibitors are available for AD?

options.text: JAK inhibitors have no safety concerns

Oral JAK inhibitors for moderate-severe AD: UPADACITINIB (Rinvoq): JAK1-selective; 15mg or 30mg daily; Rapid onset (itch improvement in days); ABROCITINIB (Cibinqo): JAK1-selective; 100mg or 200mg daily; approved ≥12 years; BARICITINIB: JAK1/2; Approved in EU, off-label in US for AD; SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS: boxed warning (class-wide from RA data): VTE, MACE, malignancy, serious infections; Require baseline and periodic lab monitoring; Consider biologics first per some guidelines (AAAAI)
No lab monitoring needed
JAK inhibitors are first-line before biologics

questions.explanation: JAK inhibitors for AD: UPADACITINIB (Rinvoq): Selective JAK1 inhibitor. 15mg or 30mg once daily. approved ≥12 years. Very rapid itch relief (within days). May be most effective JAKi based on trial data. ABROCITINIB (Cibinqo): Selective JAK1 inhibitor. 100mg or 200mg once daily. approved ≥12 years. Also rapid onset. BARICITINIB: JAK1 and JAK2 inhibitor. indicated for RA, alopecia areata (not AD in US). Approved for AD in EU and Japan. AAD gives strong recommendation (off-label). SAFETY CONSIDERATIONS: FDA BOXED WARNING (class-wide from RA studies): Increased risk of serious infections, VTE, MACE, malignancy, mortality (in RA). LAB MONITORING required: CBC, LFTs, lipids at baseline and periodically. Screen for TB, hepatitis. AAAAI guidelines: Suggest biologics before JAKi given safety profile. AAD: Strong recommendation for all three, but acknowledge safety data. Patient preference matters - oral vs. injection.

sources.text: StatPearls: Atopic Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to choose systemic therapy for this patient.
questions.question: How do you choose between biologics and JAK inhibitors?

options.text: Always start with JAK inhibitors
Choice doesn't matter - all equally safe

FACTORS favoring BIOLOGICS (dupilumab/tralokinumab): First-line per most experts; Better long-term safety data (5+ years); No lab monitoring; Comorbid asthma, CRSwNP, EoE (dupilumab approved); FACTORS favoring JAK INHIBITORS: Rapid onset needed; Oral administration preferred; Needle phobia; Prior biologic failure; Younger patients without CV risk factors; SHARED DECISION-MAKING essential; Consider: Comorbidities, patient preference, cost, access
Only dermatologists can prescribe

questions.explanation: Choosing systemic therapy: FAVOR BIOLOGICS (dupilumab, tralokinumab): Expert consensus: dupilumab as first-line. More long-term safety data (5+ years post-marketing). No lab monitoring required. Excellent for patients with comorbid atopic conditions (dupilumab approved for asthma, CRSwNP, EoE). Lower infection risk. FAVOR JAK INHIBITORS: Need for rapid symptom control (days vs. weeks). Prefer oral over injection. Needle phobia. Previous biologic failure. Potentially more effective for severe disease (higher response rates in trials). CAUTION with JAKi: Older patients (>65), CV risk factors, VTE history, malignancy history, smoking. This patient: Young, comorbid asthma and allergic rhinitis → DUPILUMAB would be an excellent choice (addresses multiple atopic diseases). Shared decision-making with patient regarding preferences is essential.

sources.text: StatPearls: Atopic Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has moderate AD with significant QoL impact, inadequately controlled on topicals.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: No treatment available for this severity
Long-term oral prednisone

Complete approach: (1) Confirm diagnosis: Chronic, pruritic, flexural dermatitis + atopic history → AD; (2) Optimize TOPICAL therapy: Daily moisturizers, mid-potency TCS for body, TCI for face; (3) Assess severity: EASI 16 (moderate), DLQI 18 (severe impact) → systemic candidate; (4) SYSTEMIC therapy: Recommend DUPILUMAB first-line (also treats her asthma/rhinitis); No labs needed, SC q2 weeks; (5) Continue topicals as adjunct; (6) Monitor: Conjunctivitis, assess response at 16 weeks; (7) Address triggers: Dust mite allergen reduction
Topical therapy alone is sufficient

questions.explanation: Complete management for this patient: DIAGNOSIS CONFIRMED: 20-year history, pruritus, flexural distribution, personal/family atopy → meets Hanifin-Rajka criteria. SEVERITY: EASI 16 (moderate), but DLQI 18 (severe QoL impact), Pruritus NRS 8/10 → significant disease burden. TOPICAL OPTIMIZATION: Liberal daily emollients. Mid-potency TCS (triamcinolone 0.1%) for body/flexures. TCI (tacrolimus 0.1%) for face, sensitive areas. Proactive maintenance therapy. SYSTEMIC THERAPY INDICATED: DUPILUMAB recommended: First-line biologic per experts, Also addresses comorbid asthma and allergic rhinitis, No lab monitoring needed, 600mg loading dose SC, then 300mg SC every 2 weeks. ADJUNCTIVE: Continue topical therapy, Counsel on conjunctivitis risk (artificial tears if needed), Allergen reduction (dust mite covers, environmental controls). FOLLOW-UP: Assess response at 16 weeks (EASI-75 goal), Monitor for side effects.

sources.text: StatPearls: Atopic Dermatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected atopic dermatitis?

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in atopic dermatitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in atopic dermatitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in atopic dermatitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

A.misconception: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.
A.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

B.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
B.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.

C.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of atopic dermatitis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

B.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
C.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.

D.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.

mcqs.stem: When managing atopic dermatitis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

C.misconception: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.
C.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.

D.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization.
correct.deeperWhy: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.
correct.clinicalPearl: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose tr
